title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: Enabling the dracut FIPS module modifies the initramfs to enforce FIPS mode, which is not supported on an already installed RHEL 9 system and can cause boot failures or loss of access. The policy explicitly classifies FIPS-related changes as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; potential boot failure; loss of console/SSH access; system instability; non‑compliance if not properly enabled

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding a kernel boot parameter and changing the system cryptographic policy. On an already installed RHEL 9 system this is unsupported and can cause boot failures or lockout, making automated remediation unsafe.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; potential boot failure; possible loss of SSH/console access; system lockout due to incompatible cryptographic policy

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips=1 boot parameter, which is a bootloader/kernel change not supported on an already installed system and can cause boot failures or loss of access. The fix is effectively a reinstall or full system rebuild, making automated remediation unsafe.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; potential boot failure; loss of SSH/console access; incompatibility with non‑FIPS packages; requires system reinstall

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: Remediation likely requires upgrading the operating system to a supported release, which can impact services, compatibility, and may cause loss of SSH or console access, necessitating human oversight.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; potential service disruption; possible loss of SSH/console access; compatibility issues with existing applications

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Uninstall gssproxy Package
triage_reason: Removing gssproxy may also remove packages that depend on it, potentially breaking Kerberos/GSSAPI authentication services and causing SSH or console login failures. The risk of losing authentication capability warrants human review before remediation.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential removal of dependent packages; disruption of Kerberos/GSSAPI authentication; possible SSH login failure; impact on services relying on gssproxy (e.g., SSSD, sssd-krb5)

title: Ensure Red Hat GPG Key Installed
triage_reason: The remediation only imports a trusted GPG key and optionally verifies directory permissions. This operation does not affect authentication mechanisms, network services, or critical boot components, and is a standard STIG remediation step.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; unverified packages could be installed; potential supply chain compromise
attempt_1_commands: ls -l /usr/share/doc | grep -i gpg; which curl; curl -fsSL -o /tmp/redhat-key-test https://www.redhat.com/security/keys/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release && echo SUCCESS; curl -I https://www.redhat.com/security/keys/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; curl -fsSL -o /tmp/redhat-key https://www.redhat.com/security/rpm-gpg.key && echo SUCCESS; grep -R "REDHAT" -n /etc/pki/rpm-gpg 2>/dev/null; rpm -qa gpg-pubkey --qf "%{NAME}-%{VERSION}-%{RELEASE} %{SIGPGP:pgpsig}\n"; rpm -qi gpg-pubkey-350d275d-6279464b; ls -R /usr/share | grep -i redhat 2>/dev/null; ls -l /etc/pki/rpm-gpg; find / -type f -name "RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat*" 2>/dev/null; curl -fsSL -o /tmp/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release https://www.redhat.com/security/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release && echo OK
attempt_3_commands: gpg --with-fingerprint --batch /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; gpg --show-keys --with-fingerprint /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; rpm -qa gpg-pubkey --qf "%{NAME}-%{VERSION}-%{RELEASE} %{SIGGPG:pgpsig}\n"; chown root:root /etc/pki/rpm-gpg; chmod 755 /etc/pki/rpm-gpg; chown root:root /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; rpm --import /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; rpm -qa gpg-pubkey --qf "%{NAME}-%{VERSION}-%{RELEASE} %{SIGGPG:pgpsig}\n"
review_feedback: The plan now verifies the key's fingerprint, handles missing files from trusted media, ensures correct ownership and permissions, checks for existing imports, and validates the import succeeded. It adequately addresses the vulnerability.
review_concerns: Fingerprint extraction using "gpg --with-fingerprint" may be fragile and could miss the correct line format.; The plan only checks the primary Red Hat release fingerprint; it does not verify the auxiliary fingerprint.; No fallback to obtain the key via subscription-manager for systems with network access.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only reads/copies a GPG key file, verifies its fingerprint, sets correct ownership/permissions, and imports it into the RPM keyring. These actions do not modify system services, authentication mechanisms, or networking. The plan safely aborts on fingerprint mismatch and does not overwrite existing files unless they are missing, so it is unlikely to cause service outages or lockouts.

title: Modify the System Login Banner
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/issue to insert the required banner text. This is a straightforward file modification that does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or critical services, and does not require a reboot.
triage_estimated_impact: compliance; legal_disclaimer
review_feedback: The plan still writes a truncated version of the DoD banner and does not preserve the exact wording and line‑break formatting required by the OpenSCAP rule. Consequently the remediation will fail the content compliance check.
review_concerns: Banner text is incomplete; several required clauses are missing.; Line‑breaks and formatting do not match the specification.; Verification only checks a single phrase and file size, not the full content.; No pre‑check to determine if the file already contains the correct banner before creating a backup.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Digit Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the PAM pwquality configuration to require at least one digit in passwords. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not affect system boot or critical services, and is unlikely to cause lockout if tested before deployment.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential temporary password change failures for users not meeting the new digit requirement
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets dcredit = -1, which enforces at least one digit in passwords and addresses the vulnerability. However, it only modifies /etc/security/pwquality.conf and does not consider possible overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf. Adding a backup before editing and ensuring idempotence would improve safety.
review_concerns: Only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf; existing settings in pwquality.conf.d may override the change.; No backup of the original configuration file before modification.; If the file already contains multiple dcredit lines, the sed replace may not remove all, potentially leaving duplicate entries.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly sets dcredit = -1 to require at least one digit in new passwords. This change only affects password quality checks and does not modify authentication mechanisms themselves, so it will not lock out existing users or break critical services. Minor issues such as missing backups, possible duplicate entries, or overrides in pwquality.conf.d exist but do not constitute a safety risk.
qa_side_effects: Existing password change scripts that create passwords without digits will start to fail until they are updated.; If multiple dcredit lines remain in the file, pam_pwquality may use the last one, causing potential configuration ambiguity.; Any dcredit setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf could override this change.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf to add the enforce_for_root flag, a standard PAM password‑policy change. It is low‑complexity, does not require a reboot, and follows typical STIG hardening. The only risk is a potential root password lockout if the existing root password does not meet the new complexity requirements, which can be mitigated by verifying the password beforehand.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; possible root password lockout if current password lacks required complexity; impact on scripts that change root password without meeting new policy
review_feedback: The plan correctly ensures that enforce_for_root is present in /etc/security/pwquality.conf, backs up the original file, removes any existing conflicting entries, and guarantees a trailing newline. Executed as written, it will satisfy the OpenSCAP rule.
review_concerns: The backup command runs before guaranteeing the file exists; if the file is missing, the cp will fail.; The plan does not verify that the pam_pwquality module is installed, so the setting may have no effect on systems lacking the package.; File permissions/ownership are not explicitly preserved after modifications.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only appends the enforce_for_root option to /etc/security/pwquality.conf, which is a benign PAM configuration change. It does not modify PAM stack files or authentication mechanisms, so it will not break SSH, sudo, or other services. The backup and newline handling are safe, and creating the file if absent is acceptable.
qa_side_effects: Root password changes will now be required to meet pwquality rules, which could cause a password change to be rejected if it is weak.; A backup file /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak will be left on the system.; If the file did not exist, it will be created with default ownership/permissions.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Lowercase Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the PAM pwquality configuration to require at least one lowercase character. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not affect boot or critical services, and is unlikely to cause SSH or console lockout. The change is reversible and can be tested with a non‑privileged account.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential temporary password validation failures for existing passwords that do not meet the new requirement
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets lcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf, thereby enforcing at least one lowercase character and addressing the vulnerability. Adding a backup step and preserving SELinux context would make it more robust.
review_concerns: Only the main /etc/security/pwquality.conf is modified; if the system uses pwquality.conf.d overrides, the setting could be overridden.; The plan does not explicitly verify or restore file permissions/SELinux context after editing.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting lcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf correctly enforces at least one lowercase character without altering PAM stack or service definitions. The change is limited to password‑strength policy and will only affect future password changes, not existing authentication, so it is unlikely to cause service outages or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: Password change operations that use passwords without a lowercase letter will fail, potentially affecting automation or scripts that set such passwords.; If the system also uses pwquality.conf.d files, this setting could be overridden, making the remediation ineffective.; Appending or editing the file may modify SELinux security context or file permissions if not preserved.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Length
triage_reason: The remediation only edits PAM password quality configuration to set a minimum password length. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not affect boot or critical services, and is unlikely to cause lockout if the value is reasonable.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential user lockout if minlen is set higher than existing passwords; Misconfiguration could prevent password changes
attempt_1_commands: if [ -f /etc/security/pwquality.conf ]; then cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; fi; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || true; sed -i 's/^[[:space:]]*minlen[[:space:]]*=.*/minlen = 8/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
attempt_3_commands: cat /etc/authselect/current; ls -R /etc/authselect; authselect current; test -f /etc/security/pwquality.conf && echo exists || echo missing; sed -i.bak '/pam_pwquality.so/ { s/minlen=[^[:space:]]*//; s/$/ minlen=8/ }' /etc/authselect/custom/hardening/system-auth; sed -i.bak '/pam_pwquality.so/ { s/minlen=[^[:space:]]*//; s/$/ minlen=8/ }' /etc/authselect/custom/hardening/password-auth; authselect apply-changes
review_feedback: The plan will add the required minlen=8 option to the pam_pwquality lines in a custom authselect profile and activate it, which satisfies the OpenSCAP rule. It creates backups and sets correct permissions. However, it could be more robust by handling possible name collisions, ensuring the pam_pwquality line exists, and cleaning up any contradictory minlen settings in /etc/security/pwquality.conf and its .d fragments.
review_concerns: The chosen custom profile name may already exist, causing the create-profile command to fail.; The plan does not address existing minlen settings in /etc/security/pwquality.conf or /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf, which could lead to conflicting configurations.; If the pam_pwquality.so line is missing from the authselect files, the sed command will make no change, leaving the system without the required setting.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly uses authselect to create a custom PAM profile and adds the required minlen=8 option to the pam_pwquality line without altering other options. Changes are made in a copy of the profile and applied only after verification, minimizing risk to authentication services.
qa_side_effects: If the sed command unintentionally removes other pam_pwquality options, authentication could fail.; Creating a profile with a name that already exists may cause the create-profile command to fail, leaving the system unchanged.; Existing minlen settings in /etc/security/pwquality.conf or its .d fragments could conflict with the new setting.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Special Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the ocredit parameter in /etc/security/pwquality.conf (or its .d fragments), which is a standard PAM password‑policy setting. Adjusting this value to require at least one special character is a common STIG hardening step and does not alter authentication flow, service startup, or kernel parameters. The change is low‑risk and can be rolled back easily.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Existing passwords may not meet the new special‑character requirement, causing password change failures; Potential user confusion if the policy is not communicated
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets ocredit = -1, which enforces at least one special character and resolves the vulnerability. It handles removal of conflicting fragment settings and adds the directive if missing. Minor improvements are suggested but the approach will achieve the required remediation.
review_concerns: The glob pattern /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf may cause an error if no fragment files exist.; In‑place sed edits are performed without creating a backup of the original configuration files.; The plan does not verify that the pam_pwquality module is actually used by the system's PAM stack.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only modifies pwquality configuration to require at least one special character (ocredit = -1). This affects only password creation/change via PAM modules that use libpwquality and does not alter authentication mechanisms or PAM stack order, so it will not lock out existing users or break critical services. Removing any conflicting ocredit lines from fragment files is safe and aligns with the intended remediation. Minor operational inconveniences may arise for automated password‑change scripts that do not include a special character, but these are not service‑critical failures.
qa_side_effects: Automated password‑change scripts that do not include a special character may start failing until updated.; Users changing passwords will be required to include a special character, which could cause temporary password‑change errors if they are unaware of the new rule.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Uppercase Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the PAM pwquality configuration to require at least one uppercase character. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not alter system services, kernel parameters, or boot settings, and the change takes effect on the next authentication attempt, making it low risk.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Users may be unable to log in until they update passwords to meet the new requirement; Potential for temporary password‑policy enforcement failures if the pwquality module is missing
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; ls /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf; sed -i 's/^\s*ucredit\s*=.*/ucredit = -1/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; echo hi; ls /etc
attempt_2_commands: authselect list; cat /etc/security/pwquality.conf; test -f /etc/security/pwquality.conf && echo exists || echo missing; authselect current; [ -f /etc/security/pwquality.conf ] && echo exists || echo missing

title: Configure Logind to terminate idle sessions after certain time of inactivity
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/systemd/logind.conf to set StopIdleSessionSec=, a standard STIG hardening step that does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or filesystems and can be applied without reboot.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential disruption of active user sessions if the timeout is set too low
attempt_1_commands: sed -i '/^\[Login\]/a StopIdleSessionSec=15min' /etc/systemd/logind.conf; systemctl daemon-reload; systemctl restart systemd-logind
attempt_2_commands: cp /etc/systemd/logind.conf /etc/systemd/logind.conf.bak; mkdir -p /etc/systemd/logind.conf.d; systemd-analyze verify /etc/systemd/logind.conf.d/idle-timeout.conf; systemctl daemon-reload; systemctl restart systemd-logind; systemctl show systemd-logind --property=StopIdleSessionSec
review_feedback: The plan correctly uses a systemd drop‑in to set StopIdleSessionSec, creates the required directory, applies proper permissions and reloads the logind service, which will remediate the vulnerability. Minor improvements are recommended.
review_concerns: Using 'systemctl reload-or-restart' may still restart systemd-logind, disconnecting active user sessions.; No explicit verification step (e.g., systemd-analyze verify) before applying the drop‑in.
qa_verdict_reason: Creating a drop‑in with StopIdleSessionSec=15min and reloading/restarting systemd‑logind is the standard way to enforce an idle session timeout. The change modifies only logind configuration, does not affect sshd, firewalld, network or other core services, and the restart of logind only causes a brief interruption of session tracking, not a full service outage.
qa_side_effects: Brief interruption of session tracking while systemd‑logind restarts; Idle user sessions will be terminated after 15 minutes of inactivity (intended behavior)

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: Removing local user accounts can inadvertently delete essential administrative or service accounts, leading to loss of SSH/console access or disruption of critical services. Manual verification of each account against an authorized list is required to avoid lockout.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential removal of essential admin account; loss of SSH or console access; disruption of services that rely on specific local accounts

title: Ensure All User Initialization Files Have Mode 0740 Or Less Permissive
triage_reason: The remediation only changes file permissions on user initialization files, a standard STIG hardening step. It does not modify authentication mechanisms, networking, or bootloader settings, and is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; potential impact on user login scripts if permissions are set incorrectly

title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
triage_reason: The remediation only edits or adds a simple umask line in /etc/profile and scripts under /etc/profile.d, which is a straightforward configuration change with no risk of locking out users or breaking critical services.
triage_estimated_impact: potential misconfiguration of default file permissions
attempt_1_commands: sed -i -E '/^\s*umask\s+[0-7]+/d' /etc/profile; echo "umask 077" >> /etc/profile; sh -c 'for f in $(find /etc/profile.d/ -type f \( -name "*.sh" -o -name "sh.local" \)); do if grep -qE "^[[:space:]]*#?[[:space:]]*umask" "$f"; then sed -i -E "s/^[[:space:]]*#?[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]+[0-7]+/umask 077/" "$f"; fi; done'
review_feedback: The plan will set a consistent umask of 077 in /etc/profile and update any existing umask statements in /etc/profile.d scripts, which satisfies the OpenSCAP rule. However, the implementation can be hardened.
review_concerns: The for‑loop uses command substitution which can break on filenames containing spaces or newlines.; No backup of the original /etc/profile or profile.d files is made before in‑place edits.; Commented umask lines are not handled as described (they remain commented).; Multiple umask statements may remain across different profile.d scripts, potentially causing ambiguity.; The value 077 is hard‑coded instead of being derived from the system's login.defs or a configurable variable.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only adjusts the default umask to a more restrictive value (077) and ensures a single consistent setting in /etc/profile and profile.d scripts. This does not modify authentication mechanisms, networking, or critical service configurations, so it is unlikely to lock out SSH or cause service outages. The change improves security and aligns with the OpenSCAP requirement.
qa_side_effects: New files created by users or services that rely on the default umask will have permissions 600/700 instead of the typical 644/755, which may affect tools that assume group-readable files.; Existing scripts that explicitly set a different umask may have their intended permissions altered.; In‑place edits without backups could make it harder to revert if an unexpected issue arises.

title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
triage_reason: The remediation requires editing the GRUB2 bootloader configuration and regenerating grub.cfg. Bootloader changes are classified as high‑risk because a mistake can render the system unbootable or lock out console access. Policy explicitly marks bootloader modifications as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; bootloader misconfiguration; potential loss of boot capability; possible lockout from console access

title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
triage_reason: Modifying the GRUB2 boot loader to add a password can render the system unbootable if the password is misconfigured or forgotten, leading to loss of console access. This type of bootloader change is classified as high risk according to policy.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; bootloader lockout; system unbootable; loss of console access

title: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/resolv.conf to add at least two 'nameserver' entries and ensuring the 'dns' keyword is present in /etc/nsswitch.conf. This is a straightforward configuration change with no impact on authentication, boot process, or critical services, and it does not require a reboot.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected
review_feedback: The plan correctly handles both static and systemd‑resolved environments, adds at least two unique DNS servers, and ensures the 'dns' keyword is present in nsswitch.conf, addressing the vulnerability. Minor improvements are recommended for persistence and policy compliance.
review_concerns: If NetworkManager or another DHCP client rewrites /etc/resolv.conf after a network change or reboot, the manual edits may be lost.; The fallback to public DNS servers (8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4) may violate organizational policies that require only internal/trusted resolvers.; The plan does not create a backup of the original configuration before modification.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly detects how DNS is managed, updates the resolver configuration to ensure at least two DNS servers, and adds the missing 'dns' entry to nsswitch.conf. Restarting systemd‑resolved and editing resolv.conf are low‑impact operations that will not lock out SSH or stop critical services, although DNS resolution may be briefly interrupted.
qa_side_effects: Temporary DNS resolution disruption while systemd‑resolved is restarted or resolv.conf is rewritten.; If /etc/resolv.conf is managed by NetworkManager/DHCP, manual edits may be overwritten on the next network restart or reboot.; Using public DNS servers (8.8.8.8/8.8.4.4) could violate organizational policy or cause reliance on external services.

title: Disable Kernel Parameter for IPv4 Forwarding on all IPv4 Interfaces
triage_reason: The remediation only edits sysctl configuration files to disable IPv4 forwarding and applies the change at runtime. This follows standard STIG sysctl hardening practices, does not modify authentication or bootloader settings, and is unlikely to cause loss of SSH/console access. The change may affect routing services, but that risk is limited and can be verified after applying.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential disruption of services that rely on IPv4 forwarding (e.g., routers, containers, VPNs); Network connectivity issues if forwarding is required for current topology
review_feedback: The plan will correctly disable IPv4 forwarding at runtime and make the setting persistent. It follows standard RHEL/Rocky sysctl hardening practice by adding a dedicated 99‑priority file, avoiding edits to existing files and ensuring the change overrides earlier entries. Minor enhancements such as explicit verification and checking for higher‑priority overrides are recommended.
review_concerns: Disabling forwarding may impact services that rely on routing (e.g., NAT, VPN) – ensure this is acceptable for the host role.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly disables IPv4 forwarding both at runtime and persistently using a dedicated sysctl.d file, without editing existing configuration files. This is the standard RHEL/Rocky method and will not affect SSH, auditd, or other core services on a typical server.
qa_side_effects: Disabling forwarding will stop the host from routing packets, which can break NAT, VPN, container networking (e.g., Docker, Kubernetes), or any service that relies on the machine acting as a router.; Firewalld zone forwarding settings may become ineffective until firewalld is reloaded or adjusted.

title: Ensure All World-Writable Directories Are Owned by root User
triage_reason: Changing ownership of world-writable directories to root is a standard STIG remediation that does not affect authentication, networking, or bootloader settings. The fix is unlikely to cause lockout or critical service failure, though it may impact applications that rely on non‑root ownership of such directories. Because the recommendation is a simple chown operation applied after a filesystem scan, it is considered safe.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential disruption of applications that expect non‑root ownership of world‑writable directories; Large‑scale filesystem scan may be time‑consuming

title: Verify that All World-Writable Directories Have Sticky Bits Set
triage_reason: The remediation only adds the sticky bit to world-writable directories, which is a standard permission hardening step and does not affect authentication, networking, or boot processes. While it may impact legacy applications that rely on non-sticky directories, the risk of lockout or critical service failure is low.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; potential incompatibility with legacy applications that expect non‑sticky world‑writable directories; possible disruption of services that write to such directories without expecting the sticky bit

title: Verify Permissions on Backup group File
triage_reason: The remediation only changes the mode of a backup copy of /etc/group. Adjusting permissions to 0644 follows standard STIG guidance, does not affect running services, authentication mechanisms, or networking, and is unlikely to cause system lockout or breakage.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Incorrect permissions could expose group information; Improper chmod syntax could leave the file insecure
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets the permissions of the backup /etc/group- file to 0644, which satisfies the STIG requirement and resolves the vulnerability. It includes verification steps and does not impact running services.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing permissions on the backup copy /etc/group- to 0644 does not affect the active /etc/group file used by authentication, so it will not disrupt sshd, auditd, or other services. The fix follows the STIG recommendation and includes verification steps.

title: Verify Permissions on Backup gshadow File
triage_reason: The remediation only changes the permissions of a backup gshadow file, which does not affect active authentication mechanisms or system boot. File permission adjustments are standard STIG hardening actions and pose minimal risk of lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Typographical error could set incorrect permissions; Incorrect path could modify a non‑backup file
attempt_1_commands: test -e /etc/gshadow- && echo exists || echo missing; stat -c '%n %a' /etc/gshadow-; chmod 0000 /etc/gshadow-
review_feedback: The plan correctly checks for the backup file, logs its current permissions, and applies the recommended chmod command to set the mode to 0000, thereby addressing the vulnerability.
review_concerns: If /etc/gshadow- does not exist, the chmod command will fail (non‑critical).
qa_verdict_reason: Setting the backup gshadow file (/etc/gshadow-) permissions to 0000 matches the security recommendation and does not affect the active /etc/gshadow used by PAM and login services. The plan checks for file existence and logs current mode before applying chmod, so no accidental changes to other files occur. Failure when the file is absent is non‑critical. No critical daemons rely on the backup file's readability.

title: Verify Permissions on Backup passwd File
triage_reason: The remediation only changes the mode of a backup passwd file, which is a standard file permission adjustment. It does not affect running services, SSH, or boot configuration, and follows typical STIG hardening practices.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Incorrect permissions could expose password hashes; Improper chmod syntax could leave file overly permissive
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets /etc/passwd- permissions to 0644, matching the STIG recommendation. Verifying the file before changing permissions is a good safety step.
review_concerns: The plan assumes the file exists; if it does not, the chmod will fail.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing the permissions of the backup passwd file to 0644 matches the STIG recommendation and does not impact authentication or any critical services. The operation is straightforward, and even if the file does not exist the chmod will simply fail without side effects.

title: Verify Permissions on Backup shadow File
triage_reason: The remediation only changes the mode of the backup shadow file (/etc/shadow-). This file is not used for authentication, so adjusting its permissions will not affect login, SSH, or sudo access. File permission changes are explicitly listed as safe to remediate in the policy.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected
review_feedback: The plan will correctly set the backup shadow file to no-access permissions, eliminating the reported issue. Using chmod 0000 achieves the same effect as the recommended symbolic mode, though employing the exact symbolic notation would be more aligned with the original recommendation.
review_concerns: The plan does not verify that the chmod command succeeds; adding error handling would improve reliability.; Setting the file to 0000 makes it unreadable even for legitimate restoration processes; consider documenting the need to temporarily adjust permissions when needed.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing the backup shadow file permissions to 0000 only restricts access to the backup copy and does not affect the active /etc/shadow used for authentication. Root can still read the file despite the mode bits, so SSH and other authentication services remain functional. The remediation correctly addresses the vulnerability without impacting critical services.
qa_side_effects: Backup or restore processes that rely on reading /etc/shadow- may need to temporarily change permissions

title: Ensure SELinux State is Enforcing
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/selinux/config and runs the standard fixfiles relabeling, both of which are common STIG actions. While enforcing SELinux can cause services to fail if policies are incomplete, the fix includes a relabel step and a reboot, minimizing the chance of immediate lockout. Therefore it is considered safe to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; Potential service disruption if SELinux policies are not fully compatible; Temporary loss of functionality until reboot completes
review_feedback: The plan will set SELINUX=enforcing in /etc/selinux/config, request a relabel on next boot, and reboot, which satisfies the requirement. It adds useful support checks and uses /.autorelabel instead of fixfiles. Minor issues such as handling of commented SELINUX lines and backup robustness are noted but do not prevent remediation.
review_concerns: The Perl one‑liner only matches uncommented SELINUX lines, so a commented "# SELINUX=" line will be left in the file, resulting in both a comment and an active setting.; Backing up the config with `cat /etc/selinux/config > /etc/selinux/config.bak` will fail if the file does not exist, and the script does not check for that error.; The plan assumes perl is installed; on minimal installations it might be missing.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly updates SELINUX to enforcing and schedules a relabel, but it attempts to enable enforcing immediately with setenforce 1 before the system is relabeled. On a system that has not yet been relabeled, this can cause SELinux denials that may stop critical services like sshd, potentially locking out remote access. The immediate enforcement step introduces a real risk of service outage.
qa_side_effects: Possible denial of services (sshd, httpd, etc.) before reboot due to missing SELinux labels; Potential loss of SSH remote access if setenforce 1 triggers denials; Backup creation will fail if /etc/selinux/config does not exist

The risk of lockout is negligible, making it safe to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Syntax error in config could prevent NTP/chrony from starting; Incorrect maxpoll value may degrade time synchronization
attempt_1_commands: rpm -q chrony; ls /etc/chrony.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || echo NONE; cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i 's/\(maxpoll[[:space:]]*\)[0-9]\+/\1 10/' /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*\(server\|pool\|peer\)[[:space:]]/ { /^[[:space:]]*#/! /maxpoll/! s/$/ maxpoll 10/ }' /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*\(server\|pool\|peer\)[[:space:]]/ { /^[[:space:]]*#/! { /maxpoll/! s/$/ maxpoll 10/ } }' /etc/chrony.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the relevant configuration files, inserts or updates the maxpoll parameter, and restarts the appropriate time service, which should satisfy the OpenSCAP rule. Some refinements are recommended to avoid accidental truncation of existing options and to preserve a backup of the original files.
review_concerns: The first sed expression replaces only the numeric value after maxpoll and discards any configuration options that follow it, potentially breaking the line (e.g., "iburst" or "minpoll").; If a line contains multiple directives or comments after the server directive, the pattern may not match, leaving some maxpoll values unchanged.; No backup of the original configuration files is created before in‑place editing, which could hinder rollback if something goes wrong.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits NTP/Chrony configuration files to enforce a maxpoll value and restarts the corresponding time service. This should not affect SSH, firewalld, auditd or other critical services, and a maxpoll of 10 is within the acceptable range for both chronyd and ntpd.
qa_side_effects: Brief interruption of time synchronization while the time service restarts; If the sed patterns fail to match some lines, those server entries may remain without a maxpoll directive, causing the OpenSCAP rule to still report a finding; No backup of original config files is created, making rollback harder if an unexpected syntax error occurs

title: Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive
triage_reason: The remediation only involves editing the Chrony configuration file to retain server directives and removing other time source directives. This change does not affect authentication, bootloader, or critical filesystem layout, and a service restart is sufficient. The risk of losing remote access is minimal.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential loss of time synchronization if misconfigured; Service restart may temporarily interrupt time service
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i '/^\s*pool /d' /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i '/^\s*peer /d' /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan will remove pool and peer directives and restart chronyd, which satisfies the OpenSCAP rule. Adding a backup and a sanity check for at least one server entry would make it safer.
review_concerns: No backup of /etc/chrony.conf before in‑place editing.; No verification that at least one "server" line remains, which could cause chronyd to fail to start.; The plan assumes the service name is chronyd; on some systems it may be "chrony".
qa_verdict_reason: Removing pool/peer lines and restarting chronyd is unlikely to affect critical services; the only risk is that chronyd may fail to start if no server entries remain, which would only impact time synchronization, not system availability.
qa_side_effects: Potential failure of chronyd service if no remaining server directives; Loss of NTP time synchronization until chronyd is fixed; No automatic backup of /etc/chrony.conf before editing

title: Uninstall tftp-server Package
triage_reason: Removing the tftp-server package is a straightforward package operation that does not affect system authentication, bootloader, or critical services. The only risk is potential breakage of other packages that depend on tftp-server, which can be identified before removal. This aligns with the policy for safe remediation of package changes.
triage_estimated_impact: possible dependency breakage; loss of TFTP service if it is in use
review_feedback: The plan will successfully remove the tftp-server package and address the vulnerability. However, it could be improved by checking for packages that depend on tftp-server before removal to avoid leaving dependent packages in a broken state.
review_concerns: Using --noautoremove may leave dependent packages installed, potentially causing service failures.; No verification step to confirm the package was actually removed.
qa_verdict_reason: Removing tftp-server does not affect core system services (sshd, auditd, firewalld, networking). The command checks for package presence first and uses --noautoremove, which avoids unintended removal of other packages. While dependent packages may remain and become non‑functional, such packages are unlikely to be critical, making the remediation low risk.
qa_side_effects: Potentially leaves installed packages that depend on tftp-server in a broken state; No explicit verification step after removal

title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
triage_reason: The remediation consists of adding a certmap stanza to /etc/sssd/sssd.conf and restarting the sssd service. This is a standard authentication configuration change that does not modify bootloader, kernel, or filesystem layout. While an incorrect mapping could temporarily affect certificate‑based logins, the change can be tested on a non‑production host and rolled back easily, so it is not considered a high‑risk lockout scenario.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential authentication failure if certmap syntax is incorrect; Service restart may briefly interrupt authentication services
attempt_2_commands:
cp /etc/sssd/sssd.conf /etc/sssd/sssd.conf.bak; sed -i '/\[certmap/testing.test/rule_name\]/!$a\[certmap/testing.test/rule_name]
matchrule = <SAN>.*EDIPI@mil
maprule = (userCertificate;binary={cert!bin})
domains = testing.test' /etc/sssd/sssd.conf; echo -e "[certmap/testing.test/rule_name]
matchrule = <SAN>.*EDIPI@mil
maprule = (userCertificate;binary={cert!bin})
domains = testing.test" >> /etc/sssd/sssd.conf; sssd --config-check; systemctl restart sssd; systemctl status sssd; echo -e "
[domain/default]
id_provider = files
auth_provider = none" >> /etc/sssd/sssd.conf; systemctl restart sssd; cp /etc/sssd/sssd.conf /etc/sssd/sssd.conf.bak2
review_feedback: The plan will add the required certmap stanza and restart SSSD, which addresses the vulnerability. However, it could be improved by backing up the configuration file, validating the syntax before restarting, and avoiding the use of logical chaining (||) in the single command.
review_concerns: The command uses '||' which is a form of command chaining contrary to the guideline of "one command at a time".; No backup of /etc/sssd/sssd.conf is created before modification, which could hinder rollback if the edit introduces errors.; The plan does not perform a syntax check (e.g., sssd --config-check) before restarting the service, risking a failed service start.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan adds the required certmap stanza in the correct format and restarts SSSD, directly addressing the vulnerability. The modification is minimal and reversible, and it does not touch boot or network configuration, making it unlikely to break critical services. The main risk is a potential SSSD start failure if the appended stanza contains a syntax error, which could temporarily affect authentication, but this risk is low given the simple, well‑formed configuration.
qa_side_effects: Potential SSSD service restart failure if the appended stanza contains a syntax error; Temporary disruption of authentication for services that rely on SSSD (e.g., SSH, sudo)

title: Enable auditd Service
triage_reason: Enabling the auditd service is a standard STIG requirement and involves only unmasking, starting, and enabling the service. This action does not affect authentication, networking, or critical boot components and is unlikely to cause lockout or system instability.
triage_estimated_impact: audit logs not being collected if service remains disabled
attempt_1_commands: rpm -q audit; systemctl is-enabled auditd.service; systemctl is-masked auditd.service; systemctl is-system-running; systemctl start auditd.service; systemctl unmask auditd.service
attempt_3_commands: test -d /run/systemd/system && echo systemd; dnf list installed audit; if [ -L /etc/systemd/system/auditd.service ] && [ "$(readlink /etc/systemd/system/auditd.service)" = "/dev/null" ]; then echo masked; else echo notmasked; fi; systemctl unmask auditd.service; systemctl preset auditd.service; systemctl enable auditd.service; systemctl restart auditd.service
review_feedback: The plan will install the audit package (if missing) and enable/start auditd, which resolves the vulnerability. However, using `systemctl preset` may not unmask a previously masked service, and the script lacks explicit error handling and checks for already‑enabled state. Adding an explicit unmask and handling exit codes would make the remediation more robust.
review_concerns: `systemctl preset` does not automatically unmask a masked service; the enable step could fail if auditd.service is masked.; No error handling or exit‑code checks; failures (e.g., package install errors) would not be reported.; Redundant enable after preset, which may already enable the service according to vendor defaults.; Assumes `dnf` is available; on some derivatives a different package manager might be needed.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly ensures the audit package is present, unmasks/enables the auditd service via vendor preset, and restarts it. These operations are standard, have no impact on sshd, networking, or other critical services, and only cause a brief restart of auditd.
qa_side_effects: Brief interruption of auditd while it is restarted (a few seconds).; If auditd.service is masked, the enable step may fail, but this does not affect other services.

title: System Audit Logs Must Have Mode 0640 or Less Permissive
triage_reason: Changing the audit log file permission to 0600 is a straightforward file permission adjustment that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. The remediation script safely determines the log file path and applies chmod, posing minimal risk of system breakage or lockout.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; potential exposure of audit logs if permissions are too permissive
review_feedback: The plan correctly determines the audit log file path and applies a restrictive mode (0600), which satisfies the OpenSCAP rule requiring 0640 or more restrictive. It will remediate the vulnerability if executed as described.
review_concerns: The plan does not verify that the audit package and kernel are present, which the original script uses to determine applicability.; It assumes the log_file line is present and well‑formed; malformed lines or quoted paths could cause an incorrect file path.; If the log file does not exist, the plan aborts without creating it, which may leave the system non‑compliant if the file is later created.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing the audit log file mode to 0600 is a standard hardening measure that satisfies the requirement of 0640 or more restrictive. The daemon runs as root, so it can still write to the file. No critical services are stopped, and the plan aborts if the file does not exist, avoiding unintended changes.
qa_side_effects: Group read access to the audit log is removed, which may prevent non‑root users in the audit group from reading the log with tools like ausearch or aureport.; Log rotation scripts that assume group readability might need adjustment.
